title: Mather-Thurston’s theory, non abelian Poincare duality and diffeomorphism groups
speaker: Sam Nariman
abstract: I will discuss a remarkable generalization of Mather’s theorem by Thurston that relates the identity component of diffeomorphism groups to the classifying space of Haefliger structures. The homotopy type of this classifying space played a fundamental role in foliation theory. However, it is notoriously difficult to determine its homotopy groups. Mather-Thurston theory relates the homology of diffeomorphism groups to these homotopy groups. Hence, this h-principle type theorem has been used as the main tool to get at the homotopy groups of Haefliger spaces.
tags: Workshop on the h-principle and beyond

proof_stages.label: Orientation and Haefliger-space conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames Thurston’s result as a reversal of the usual h-principle philosophy, defines the Haefliger classifying space BΓ_q, explains its role in classifying foliations, and states Haefliger’s conjecture that the homotopy fiber BΓ_q is 2q-connected.

proof_stages.label: Mather-Thurston reformulation via diffeomorphism groups
proof_stages.summary: Flat M-bundles are identified with BDiff^δ(M), ordinary M-bundles with BDiff(M), and the homotopy fiber of the forgetful map is packaged as a foliated-M functor satisfying a homological c-principle with formal target a section space.

proof_stages.label: Cobordism-to-flat formulation and low-degree evidence
proof_stages.summary: Haefliger’s conjecture is translated into a statement that the Mather-Thurston map is a homology isomorphism in degrees below dim M, equivalently that M-bundles over sufficiently low-dimensional bases are cobordant to flat bundles, with known H_1/H_2 and rational H_3 evidence reviewed.

proof_stages.label: Bounded homeomorphism cases and compact-versus-open contrast
proof_stages.summary: Homeomorphism and bounded-cohomology variants are discussed to emphasize the main methodological issue: Thurston proved the compactly supported c-principle before the local open-manifold version was known, contrary to the usual h-principle expectation.

proof_stages.label: Abstract Thurston criterion for compact c-principles
proof_stages.summary: Thurston’s method is abstracted to a theorem for a natural transformation F→F_formal: if the formal value on R^n is sufficiently connected and F is a good functor with inclusion homology invariance, disk-boundary acyclicity, and fragmentation, then compact manifolds satisfy the desired homology isomorphism.

proof_stages.label: Fragmentation and epsilon-supported section filtration
proof_stages.summary: The fragmentation idea is modeled on writing isotopically trivial diffeomorphisms as products supported in a chosen open cover, then implemented for section spaces by restricting to sections whose support is covered by many very small balls, giving a filtration by the number of balls.

proof_stages.label: Spectral-sequence comparison of filtration quotients
proof_stages.summary: The filtered foliated and formal functors are compared through their filtration quotients; identifying the quotient homologies yields a spectral-sequence argument proving the compactly supported homology isomorphism.

proof_stages.label: Contact and transverse-structure applications
proof_stages.summary: The abstract Thurston strategy is applied to contact transverse structures and a related transverse-linear/topological version, giving compactly supported c-principle theorems even where the corresponding local versions remain unknown.

examples.name: Circle bundles, Euler class, and Milnor-Wood
examples.note: The case of circle bundles, with BHomeo(S^1)≃CP^∞ and the Milnor-Wood bound on the Euler class of flat bundles over surfaces, is used to motivate the bounded-cohomology version of Thurston’s theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes to circle bundles, identifies their classification by the Euler class, applies the Milnor-Wood inequality to rule out flat structures with large Euler evaluation, and explains Thurston’s cobordism consequence

passes.reason: Ordinary circle bundles with too-large Euler class are used via Milnor-Wood as an obstruction to a flat structure on the fixed surface.
ordinary circle bundles over surfaces are used via Milnor-Wood as an obstruction to flatness when the Euler class is too large.

examples.name: Homeomorphisms of the real line and bounded perfectness
examples.note: Homeo(R) is used to make the H_1 statement concrete: vanishing reduced bounded homology corresponds to every element being a product of a uniformly bounded number of commutators, illustrating why open manifolds are harder.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the bounded homology statement to Homeo(R) and derives the interpretation that H1 gives bounded perfectness as products of a fixed number of commutators

passes.reason: Homeomorphisms of the real line are used as a positive instance of bounded perfectness, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Symplectic non-squeezing obstruction
examples.note: In response to a question, the symplectic case is contrasted with the contact case because non-squeezing obstructs the small-into-large homological embedding property used in the method.
examples.engagement_reason: symplectic non-squeezing is invoked as the obstruction explaining why the symplectic case is hard, without any concrete non-squeezing calculation or example

passes.reason: Symplectic non-squeezing is cited as an obstruction to the analogous symplectic construction.

Symplectic non-squeezing uses highly regular ball/cylinder domains as an obstruction to the desired symplectic local argument.
